1:
On Acts 12:1 — “Now about that time Herod the king stretched forth his hands to vex certain of the church” — the witnesses mark the new persecution.

The Eastern fathers note the rhythm of trial. Chrysostom: “mark… how things are blended, how rest and trouble alternate.” The grammatical-historical commentators identify the king. Robertson dates it “the early part of A.D. 44… the date of Herod’s death”; Ellicott and Wesley identify him as Herod Agrippa, grandson of Herod the Great, brother of Herodias. The Reformed Calvin: “the Church had some short truce… that it might then fight afresh” — the faithful must not think one or two conflicts the end.

The verse opens a fresh assault on the church, now from a king. After a season of relative peace, Herod Agrippa stretches out his hand to harass the believers. The persecution that had slackened since Saul’s conversion resumes, this time from the civil power.

So the witnesses see the church enter a new trial after a respite. The witnesses note the pattern Chrysostom and Calvin observe: God blends rest and persecution, granting his people short truces to gather strength before the next conflict. The persecutor is now Herod Agrippa, the Roman-appointed king, who turns the power of the state against the disciples. The Puritan Henry recalls that since Saul’s conversion the priests had been quieter; now the assault comes from a higher quarter. The verse thus opens the chapter’s drama: the church, lately at peace, faces a royal persecutor who will martyr one apostle and imprison another. Yet, as the chapter will show, the king who stretches out his hand against the church will himself be struck down by the hand of God — while the word of God grows the more. The trial is real, but its outcome will display God’s power over kings.

So the witnesses see the church enter a new trial after a respite. The pattern Chrysostom and Calvin observe holds: God blends rest and persecution, granting short truces to gather strength before the next conflict. The persecutor is now Herod Agrippa, the Roman-appointed king, who turns the power of the state against the disciples. Yet, as the chapter will show, the king who stretches out his hand against the church will himself be struck down by the hand of God — while the word of God grows the more. The trial is real, but its outcome will display God’s power over kings.
2:
On Acts 12:2 — “And he killed James the brother of John with the sword” — the witnesses mark the first apostle martyred.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify James and the manner of death. Robertson: James, son of Zebedee, the “son of thunder,” for whom Jesus predicted a bloody death (Mark 10:38); “the first of the apostles to die,” not James the Lord’s brother. Ellicott and Lightfoot: decapitation by the sword marked a sentence by the civil ruler in Roman fashion, not stoning by the Sanhedrin. Wesley: “one of the brothers went to God the first, the other” (John) “the last of the apostles.”

The verse records the martyrdom of James. Herod has him beheaded — the first of the twelve to die for Christ. The son of Zebedee, who had asked to sit at Christ’s right hand, drinks the cup his Lord foretold.

So the witnesses see in James’s death the fulfilment of Christ’s own prophecy. The witnesses recall that James and John had sought the chief places and been told they would indeed drink Christ’s cup and share his baptism; now James fulfils that word, the first apostle to seal his testimony with his blood. They note the manner — the sword, beheading — as the mark of a civil, Roman-style execution by the king, distinct from the Jewish stoning that had killed Stephen. The witnesses observe the sober brevity of the record: the death of an apostle told in a single line, the martyr’s glory left to God. They mark Wesley’s touching contrast — James first, John last, of the brothers who had stood so close to the Lord. The verse thus shows that the new persecution drew real blood: an apostle slain, the church bereft of a leader — and yet, as the chapter unfolds, the cause is not crushed but grows.

So the witnesses see in James’s death the fulfilment of Christ’s own prophecy. James and John had sought the chief places and been told they would drink Christ’s cup; now James fulfils that word, the first apostle to seal his testimony with his blood. The witnesses note the manner — the sword, beheading — as the mark of a civil, Roman-style execution, distinct from the Jewish stoning that killed Stephen. The death of an apostle is told in a single sober line, the martyr’s glory left to God; yet the cause is not crushed but grows.
3:
On Acts 12:3 — “And because he saw it pleased the Jews, he proceeded further to take Peter also. (Then were the days of unleavened bread.)” — the witnesses mark Herod’s motive.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the king’s policy. Barnes: he acted “not from a sense of right… but… to promote his own popularity.” Ellicott: “the persecution did not spring from any fanatic zeal… but simply from motives of political expediency” — the ruling Herodian policy. Robertson notes the Hebraism “proceeded to seize.” The Reformed Calvin: Herod “might provide for his own private affairs… that he might win the people’s favour.”

The verse lays bare why Peter was next seized. Seeing that James’s death pleased the Jews, Herod, ever the politician, takes Peter also to curry favour. The persecution springs not from conviction but from the calculation of popularity.

So the witnesses see the cynicism of the persecuting king. Herod kills not from zeal for the law or hatred of the gospel but to please the crowd; the murder of James having won him favour, he reaches for Peter to win more. The witnesses note that this exposes the base motive behind much persecution — the love of popularity, the willingness to shed innocent blood for political advantage. They mark the providential timing: it was the feast of unleavened bread, the Passover season, so that Herod would defer the public execution until the feast was past, giving space for the church’s prayers and God’s deliverance. The verse thus shows the church’s chief apostle in the gravest danger, seized by a king who would shed his blood for applause. Yet the very delay Herod’s policy required becomes the window in which God will act — turning the persecutor’s calculation into the occasion of a miracle.

So the witnesses see the cynicism of the persecuting king. Herod kills not from zeal but to please the crowd; the murder of James having won him favour, he reaches for Peter to win more. The witnesses note that this exposes the base motive behind much persecution — the love of popularity, the willingness to shed innocent blood for advantage. They mark the providential timing: the feast of unleavened bread deferred the public execution, giving space for the church’s prayers and God’s deliverance. The persecutor’s calculation becomes the window in which God will act.
4:
On Acts 12:4 — “And when he had apprehended him, he put him in prison, and delivered him to four quaternions of soldiers to keep him; intending after Easter to bring him forth to the people” — the witnesses mark the heavy guard.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the custody. Robertson: “four quaternions” (sixteen soldiers), four in each watch — Peter’s “third imprisonment” (Acts 4:3; Acts 5:18). Ellicott: the four companies relieved each other, the prisoner chained to two soldiers. Clarke calls “after Easter” an “absurd” mistranslation of “after the Passover” (meta to pascha). The Reformed Calvin: Peter was “as it were, shut up in his grave… quite past hope.”

The verse describes the security around Peter. Sixteen soldiers in four watches guard him, that he may be brought out for public execution once the Passover is over. Herod takes every precaution against escape; humanly, there is no hope.

So the witnesses see the apostle hedged about with the full force of the state. The witnesses note the extraordinary precautions — sixteen soldiers, four to a watch, Peter chained between two of them — a security that made escape impossible by any human means, and so set the stage for a deliverance manifestly divine. They mark Clarke’s well-known correction of the King James “Easter”: the Greek is “the Passover,” a Jewish, not a Christian, festival. They observe Calvin’s image of Peter “shut up in his grave,” past all hope — the very extremity in which God delights to act. The verse thus heightens the suspense: the chief apostle, condemned to die, guarded beyond all possibility of flight, awaiting only the end of the feast. The heavier the guard, the greater will be the glory of the rescue; God’s deliverances are often timed to the last and most hopeless hour.

So the witnesses see the apostle hedged about with the full force of the state — sixteen soldiers, four to a watch, Peter chained between two — a security that made escape impossible by human means, and so set the stage for a deliverance manifestly divine. They mark Clarke’s correction of the King James “Easter”: the Greek is “the Passover.” They observe Calvin’s image of Peter shut up in his grave, past all hope — the very extremity in which God delights to act. The heavier the guard, the greater the glory of the rescue, timed to the last and most hopeless hour.
5:
On Acts 12:5 — “Peter therefore was kept in prison: but prayer was made without ceasing of the church unto God for him” — the witnesses mark the church at prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the earnest prayer. Robertson: “prayer was made earnestly” (ektenos), continuously. Clarke: the word “signifies both fervour and earnestness, as well as perseverance.” Ellicott: the church “continued… to meet as usual… in the house of Mary.” The Puritan Henry marks the deliverance as “a signal answer to prayer.” The Reformed Calvin: “all the rest fought with their prayers together with him.”

The verse sets the church’s prayer against Herod’s power. While Peter is held fast, the church prays for him without ceasing, fervently and as one. The contrast is drawn: the king’s soldiers on one side, the praying church on the other.

So the witnesses see the church’s great weapon brought to bear: earnest, unceasing prayer. The witnesses note the force of the word — prayer offered fervently, intently, with persevering continuance; the whole church, Calvin says, fighting alongside the imprisoned apostle by their supplications. Against Herod’s sixteen guards stands the praying community, their only and sufficient recourse. The witnesses mark Henry’s emphasis that Peter’s deliverance was a signal answer to this prayer, demonstrating that God hears even in the most hopeless circumstances. The verse thus draws the decisive contrast of the chapter: not soldiers against soldiers, but the prayer of the church against the power of the king — and the prayer prevails. It teaches that when the saints can do nothing else, they can pray; and that the God who seems to look on while James dies will, in answer to importunate prayer, deliver Peter from the very jaws of death.

So the witnesses see the church’s great weapon brought to bear: earnest, unceasing prayer. The word means prayer offered fervently, intently, with persevering continuance; the whole church, Calvin says, fighting alongside the imprisoned apostle. Against Herod’s sixteen guards stands the praying community, their only and sufficient recourse. The witnesses mark Henry’s emphasis that Peter’s deliverance was a signal answer to this prayer. When the saints can do nothing else, they can pray; and the God who seemed to look on while James died will, in answer to importunate prayer, deliver Peter from the jaws of death.
6:
On Acts 12:6 — “And when Herod would have brought him forth, the same night Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, bound with two chains: and the keepers before the door kept the prison” — the witnesses mark the apostle’s calm sleep.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on Peter’s repose. Ellicott: “the calm repose of the Apostle… the sleep of His beloved” (Psalm 127:2), “undisturbed by the fear of coming… death.” Clarke and Robertson detail the chaining — bound to a soldier on each side. The Reformed Calvin: God “taketh his time oftentimes in the last” extremity. The Puritan Henry: “a peaceful conscience, a lively hope… can keep men calm in the full prospect of death.”

The verse sets the scene on the eve of execution. The very night before Herod meant to bring him out, Peter sleeps peacefully, chained between two soldiers, the guards at the door. The apostle rests in faith while death stands ready.

So the witnesses marvel at Peter’s tranquil sleep in the shadow of the gallows. The witnesses note the wonder of it: bound with two chains, soldiers on either side, execution fixed for the morning — yet Peter sleeps, the sleep God gives his beloved, undisturbed by fear. They mark Henry’s point that a peaceful conscience, a lively hope, and the comforts of the Spirit can keep a man calm even in the prospect of death. The contrast is striking: Herod plotting, the guards watching, the church praying through the night — and Peter, the one in deadly peril, sleeping in peace. The witnesses observe Calvin’s theme that God times his help to the last hour; the deliverance comes the very night before the execution. The verse thus pictures the serenity of faith and the imminence of the rescue: the apostle’s calm rest is itself a testimony, and the dawn that Herod meant for death God has appointed for deliverance.

So the witnesses marvel at Peter’s tranquil sleep in the shadow of the gallows. Bound with two chains, soldiers on either side, execution fixed for the morning — yet Peter sleeps, the sleep God gives his beloved, undisturbed by fear. They mark Henry’s point that a peaceful conscience, a lively hope, and the comforts of the Spirit can keep a man calm even in the prospect of death. The contrast is striking: Herod plotting, the guards watching, the church praying — and Peter, the one in deadly peril, sleeping in peace. The dawn Herod meant for death God has appointed for deliverance.
7:
On Acts 12:7 — “And, behold, the angel of the Lord came upon him, and a light shined in the prison: and he smote Peter on the side… and his chains fell off from his hands” — the witnesses mark the angelic deliverance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the heavenly visitant. Robertson: “stood by him” (epeste), the same word for the angel’s appearing to the shepherds (Luke 2:9); the “light” the accompaniment of heavenly beings (Acts 5:19). Clarke: the angel smote him “just sufficient to awake him,” and “his chains fell off.” The Reformed Calvin: perhaps Peter alone saw the light, the soldiers being “amazed” or in deep sleep.

The verse records the moment of rescue. An angel stands by Peter, the cell fills with light, a touch wakes him, and his chains fall away. The God to whom the church prayed sends his messenger to set the apostle free.

So the witnesses see the prayer of the church answered by an angel. The witnesses note the gentleness and power together: a light in the dark cell, a touch to rouse the sleeping apostle, and the chains dropping from his hands of their own accord. The same angelic ministry that had freed the apostles before (Acts 5:19) now frees Peter from a far stricter confinement. They mark Calvin’s suggestion that the soldiers, though chained to Peter, perceived nothing — God blinding their senses while delivering his servant. The verse thus shows the direct intervention of heaven on behalf of the imperilled church: the king’s chains are no obstacle to the angel of the Lord. The deliverance begins quietly, in the dead of night, with the falling of the chains — the first sign that the God who permitted James to die has decreed that Peter shall live, and that the prayers of the saints have prevailed.

So the witnesses see the prayer of the church answered by an angel. The gentleness and power together: a light in the dark cell, a touch to rouse the sleeping apostle, and the chains dropping of their own accord. The same angelic ministry that had freed the apostles before (Acts 5:19) now frees Peter from a far stricter confinement. They mark Calvin’s suggestion that the soldiers perceived nothing — God blinding their senses while delivering his servant. The deliverance begins quietly, in the dead of night, with the falling of the chains — the first sign that the God who permitted James to die has decreed that Peter shall live.
8:
On Acts 12:8 — “And the angel said unto him, Gird thyself, and bind on thy sandals… Cast thy garment about thee, and follow me” — the witnesses mark the unhurried deliverance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the deliberate calm. Clarke: Peter’s resuming all he had laid off was “a proof that everything had been done leisurely… no evidence of any hurry.” Barnes and Ellicott: he had laid aside cloak, girdle, and sandals to sleep, and is now told to dress as usual. Robertson notes the middle imperatives — gird thyself, bind on, cast about.

The verse shows the angel directing Peter step by step. He is told to dress fully — gird his tunic, put on his sandals, throw his cloak about him — and follow. There is no haste, no scramble; the deliverance proceeds with calm order.

So the witnesses note the leisurely, orderly manner of the rescue. The witnesses mark Clarke’s observation that the angel bids Peter dress himself fully and unhurriedly — girding, sandalling, cloaking — as a man rising at ease, not a prisoner fleeing in panic. This calm deliberateness, they observe, is itself a testimony: God’s deliverance owes nothing to human haste or stealth, but proceeds with the unhurried sovereignty of one wholly in command. There is no suggestion of Peter escaping by his own contrivance; he simply obeys the angel’s directions. The verse thus underscores that the rescue is entirely God’s work, accomplished with the composure of omnipotence. Peter, half-believing it a dream, dresses and follows, while the angel leads — the whole scene marked by a quiet order that befits a deliverance wrought not by flight but by the power of heaven, which need never hurry.

So the witnesses note the leisurely, orderly manner of the rescue. Clarke observes that the angel bids Peter dress himself fully and unhurriedly — girding, sandalling, cloaking — as a man rising at ease, not a prisoner fleeing in panic. This calm deliberateness is itself a testimony: God’s deliverance owes nothing to human haste or stealth, but proceeds with the unhurried sovereignty of one wholly in command. There is no suggestion of Peter escaping by his own contrivance; he simply obeys. The whole scene is marked by a quiet order befitting a deliverance wrought by the power of heaven, which need never hurry.
9:
On Acts 12:9 — “And he went out, and followed him; and wist not that it was true which was done by the angel; but thought he saw a vision” — the witnesses mark Peter’s half-belief.

The grammatical-historical commentators note his dazed state. Robertson: “wist not” (knew not), “kept on following” the angel; “true” in the sense of reality. Clarke: “he supposed himself to be in a dream.” Ellicott reads the “introspective analysis” as a sign that Peter himself was Luke’s informant — the will directing the body while “only half-conscious.” The Reformed Calvin: he thought it “a vision,” not a deception of Satan, but doubting it was real.

The verse records Peter’s dreamlike following. He goes out after the angel, yet cannot believe it is really happening; he thinks he sees a vision. The deliverance is so sudden and wonderful that the apostle’s mind cannot grasp its reality.

So the witnesses see in Peter’s confusion the very mark of a deliverance beyond expectation. The witnesses note the lifelike detail: Peter obeys and follows, his body moving while his mind, dazed by the wonder, supposes it all a vision — the same astonishment that had met the women’s report of the resurrection, when the news seemed too good to be real. They mark Ellicott’s suggestion that this inward analysis points to Peter himself as the source of the account, recalling his own half-conscious state. The witnesses observe that the very implausibility of the rescue — so far beyond hope that the rescued man doubts it — testifies to its divine character; no human escape would have left the escaper unsure it had happened. The verse thus captures the wonder of God’s answer to prayer: a deliverance so complete and unexpected that Peter, walking free past his guards, can scarcely believe he is awake.

So the witnesses see in Peter’s confusion the very mark of a deliverance beyond expectation. The lifelike detail: Peter obeys and follows, his body moving while his mind, dazed by the wonder, supposes it all a vision — the same astonishment that met the women’s report of the resurrection. They mark Ellicott’s suggestion that this inward analysis points to Peter himself as the source of the account. The very implausibility of the rescue — so far beyond hope that the rescued man doubts it — testifies to its divine character; no human escape would have left the escaper unsure it had happened.
10:
On Acts 12:10 — “When they were past the first and the second ward, they came unto the iron gate… which opened to them of his own accord” — the witnesses mark the gate that opened itself.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the route. Barnes and Robertson: the “first and second ward” were the guard-posts passed unhindered; the “iron gate” leading to the city opened of itself. Gill notes the circumstances “make the deliverance the more wonderful.” Wesley: “the gate opened of its own accord… without either Peter or the angel touching it,” and the angel led him one street, then departed. The Reformed Calvin: God “overcometh diverse straits one after another, that the glory of the miracle might be the greater.”

The verse follows Peter past every barrier. Guard-posts are passed, and the great iron gate to the city swings open of its own accord; they go out, and the angel, having led him along one street, leaves him. Every obstacle yields without human effort.

So the witnesses see the obstacles fall away one by one before the power of God. The witnesses note the mounting wonder Gill marks: ward after ward passed, and finally the massive iron gate opening untouched, as if heaven itself unbarred the way. They observe Calvin’s point that God could have whisked Peter out in an instant, but chose to lead him through successive barriers, each yielding, that the glory of the miracle might be the greater — as he made the world in six days, not from need of time but for the fuller display of his work. They mark that the angel leads Peter just far enough to set him safely on his way, then departs, his task done. The verse thus completes the escape with its crowning sign — the self-opening gate — and shows the deliverance accomplished entirely by God: no lock picked, no guard bribed, no wall scaled, but every door opened by the hand that answers prayer.

So the witnesses see the obstacles fall away one by one before the power of God. The mounting wonder Gill marks: ward after ward passed, and finally the massive iron gate opening untouched, as if heaven itself unbarred the way. They observe Calvin’s point that God could have whisked Peter out in an instant, but chose to lead him through successive barriers, each yielding, that the glory of the miracle might be the greater. The angel leads Peter just far enough to set him safely on his way, then departs. No lock picked, no guard bribed, no wall scaled, but every door opened by the hand that answers prayer.
11:
On Acts 12:11 — “And when Peter was come to himself, he said, Now I know of a surety, that the Lord hath sent his angel, and hath delivered me out of the hand of Herod” — the witnesses mark Peter’s recognition.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the awakening. Robertson: “come to himself” (en heauto genomenos), like the prodigal coming to himself; “now I know of a truth” — the confusion gone. Barnes: when he “had overcome his amazement… so as to be capable of reflection.” Ellicott hears “the tone of a personal reminiscence.” The Reformed Calvin: his words “contain a thanksgiving… he extolleth… the benefit of God.”

The verse records the moment Peter grasps the reality. Standing free in the street, the dream dispelled, he knows for certain that the Lord has sent his angel and rescued him from Herod. Wonder gives way to grateful recognition of God’s hand.

So the witnesses see Peter come fully to himself and confess the deliverance as God’s. The witnesses note the turn from the dreamlike daze to clear conviction: now, alone and free in the night street, he knows “of a surety” that it was no vision but a real rescue, wrought by the Lord’s angel against the worst Herod and the Jews intended. They mark Calvin’s point that his words are a thanksgiving, ascribing all to God. The witnesses observe Ellicott’s sense of personal reminiscence — Peter recalling the very moment of recognition. The verse thus marks the apostle’s full awareness and his immediate ascription of glory to God: he does not credit his own courage or fortune but the Lord who sent the angel. It is the proper response to deliverance — to know it as God’s work and to give him thanks — and it prepares Peter to go and tell the praying church that their prayers have been answered.

So the witnesses see Peter come fully to himself and confess the deliverance as God’s. The turn from the dreamlike daze to clear conviction: now, alone and free in the night street, he knows “of a surety” that it was no vision but a real rescue, wrought by the Lord’s angel against the worst Herod intended. They mark Calvin’s point that his words are a thanksgiving, ascribing all to God. It is the proper response to deliverance — to know it as God’s work and to give him thanks — and it prepares Peter to go and tell the praying church that their prayers have been answered.
12:
On Acts 12:12 — “And when he had considered the thing, he came to the house of Mary the mother of John, whose surname was Mark; where many were gathered together praying” — the witnesses mark the praying house.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the house. Barnes and Ellicott: Mary’s house, the home of John Mark — probably the evangelist, Peter’s “son” (1 Peter 5:13) and cousin of Barnabas. Robertson: “considered” (synidon), grasping the matter as a whole. The Puritan Henry: “these Christians continued in prayer for Peter… men ought always to pray, and not to faint.” The Reformed Calvin: Mary was “a matron of rare godliness, whose house was… a temple of God.”

The verse brings Peter to the gathered church. Having reflected, he goes to Mary’s house, where many believers are assembled in prayer — praying, it seems, for the very deliverance now accomplished. The freed apostle seeks out the praying community.

So the witnesses see the threads of the chapter drawn together: the delivered apostle comes to the house where the church is praying for him. The witnesses note the godly household — Mary’s home a meeting-place for the saints, her son John Mark the future evangelist and Peter’s spiritual son. They mark the touching providence: while the church prayed through the night, God was already answering; and Peter arrives at the door even as the prayers ascend. Henry presses the lesson of perseverance — the saints continued in prayer, not fainting. The witnesses observe that Peter’s first instinct, once free, was to find his praying brethren and share the good news. The verse thus shows the church at its proper work — gathered, persevering in prayer — and the answer to that prayer standing at the gate. It is a picture of the praying community and the faithfulness of God, who hears and answers even as his people seek him.

So the witnesses see the threads of the chapter drawn together: the delivered apostle comes to the house where the church is praying for him. The witnesses note the godly household — Mary’s home a meeting-place for the saints, her son John Mark the future evangelist. They mark the touching providence: while the church prayed through the night, God was already answering; and Peter arrives even as the prayers ascend. Henry presses the lesson of perseverance. Peter’s first instinct, once free, was to find his praying brethren — a picture of the praying community and the faithfulness of God, who hears even as his people seek him.
13:
On Acts 12:13 — “And as Peter knocked at the door of the gate, a damsel came to hearken, named Rhoda” — the witnesses mark the servant girl at the door.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the homely detail. Barnes and Ellicott: “Rhoda” (a Greek name, “rose”), a servant girl who came to the door — the naming of the slave shows “Luke’s care in ascertaining details.” Robertson: Peter knocked at the door of the gateway (pylon), the passage from the street. Clarke: the door was shut “for fear of the Jews.”

The verse turns to a small but vivid scene. Peter knocks at the outer gate, and a servant girl named Rhoda comes to see who is there. The narrative pauses, with characteristic detail, on the humble figure who first hears the apostle’s voice.

So the witnesses note the lifelike particularity of the account. The witnesses mark that Luke preserves even the name of the servant girl — Rhoda — a touch that bespeaks careful, eyewitness-rooted reporting and the dignity Scripture gives to the lowly. They observe that the door was kept shut for fear of the Jews, the church meeting in guarded secrecy during the persecution; and that it was a slave, not a master, who came to answer the knock, as was the custom. The verse thus opens the charming domestic scene that follows — Rhoda’s joy, the church’s disbelief — and grounds the great miracle in the homely reality of a knock at a gate and a girl coming to listen. It reminds us that God’s wonders unfold in the midst of ordinary life, and that the inspired record does not disdain the small and humble details, naming even the servant who first heard the freed apostle at the door.

So the witnesses note the lifelike particularity of the account. Luke preserves even the name of the servant girl — Rhoda — a touch that bespeaks careful, eyewitness-rooted reporting and the dignity Scripture gives to the lowly. They observe that the door was kept shut for fear of the Jews, the church meeting in guarded secrecy; and that a slave, not a master, came to answer the knock, as was the custom. It reminds us that God’s wonders unfold in the midst of ordinary life, and that the inspired record does not disdain the small and humble details, naming even the servant who first heard the freed apostle at the door.
14:
On Acts 12:14 — “And when she knew Peter’s voice, she opened not the gate for gladness, but ran in, and told how Peter stood before the gate” — the witnesses mark Rhoda’s joyful haste.

The grammatical-historical commentators relish the human touch. Robertson: “for joy” (apo tes charas) — a “life-like picture of the maid who left Peter standing outside” in her excitement; she “knew Peter and his voice from his frequent visits.” Ellicott: “the eager desire to tell the good news… overpowers her presence of mind” — “characteristic of the writer.” Barnes: in her joy she hastened to tell the others.

The verse captures Rhoda’s overflowing gladness. Recognising Peter’s voice, she is so overjoyed that she forgets to open the gate and runs in to announce him — leaving the apostle standing outside. Joy outruns presence of mind.

So the witnesses smile at the delightful realism of the scene. The witnesses note the lifelike detail Luke preserves: Rhoda, recognising the familiar voice of one who often visited, is so transported with joy that she leaves Peter knocking in the street while she rushes to tell the others. They mark Ellicott’s observation that this little touch — gladness overpowering good sense — is characteristic of Luke’s eye for human nature. The verse thus brings warmth and reality to the miracle: the great deliverance is met not with solemn ceremony but with the spontaneous, slightly comic joy of a servant girl too happy to open the door. It testifies, in its small way, to the genuineness of the account — no inventor would add such a homely flourish — and to the gladness that the answer to prayer brings. The praying church is about to learn, through Rhoda’s breathless report, that God has done the very thing they asked.

So the witnesses smile at the delightful realism of the scene. Rhoda, recognising the familiar voice of one who often visited, is so transported with joy that she leaves Peter knocking in the street while she rushes to tell the others. They mark Ellicott’s observation that this little touch — gladness overpowering good sense — is characteristic of Luke’s eye for human nature. The great deliverance is met not with solemn ceremony but with the spontaneous, slightly comic joy of a servant girl too happy to open the door — a homely flourish testifying to the genuineness of the account and the gladness that answered prayer brings.
15:
On Acts 12:15 — “And they said unto her, Thou art mad. But she constantly affirmed that it was even so. Then said they, It is his angel” — the witnesses mark the church’s disbelief.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the irony. Robertson: “thou art mad” (maine) — “a curious rebuff from those who had been praying all night for Peter’s release.” Barnes: “this was the very thing for which they had been… praying,” yet they would not believe it granted. On “his angel,” Clarke, Ellicott, and Wesley note the Jewish belief in a guardian angel taking the man’s likeness — though Scripture is “silent” on it (Wesley).

The verse exposes the weakness of the church’s faith. They tell Rhoda she is mad; and when she insists, they suppose it must be Peter’s angel, not Peter himself. They had prayed for his release, yet cannot believe it has come.

So the witnesses note, with gentle candour, the imperfect faith of the praying church. The witnesses mark the striking irony Robertson and Barnes press: the very people who had prayed all night for Peter’s deliverance refuse to believe it when it comes, calling the messenger mad and grasping at the notion of a guardian angel rather than the simple fact. They observe that this exposes how weak faith often is even in earnest prayer — the saints asked, but scarcely expected to receive. The witnesses note the popular Jewish belief in a guardian angel bearing the man’s likeness, which Scripture neither affirms nor builds upon. The verse thus tempers the chapter’s triumph with a humbling realism: God answers prayer more readily than his people believe, and the deliverance outran the faith that sought it. It is a comfort to weak believers — that God hears even when our faith falters — and a rebuke to the unbelief that prays yet does not look to receive.

So the witnesses note, with gentle candour, the imperfect faith of the praying church. The striking irony Robertson and Barnes press: the very people who had prayed all night for Peter’s deliverance refuse to believe it when it comes, calling the messenger mad and grasping at a guardian angel rather than the simple fact. This exposes how weak faith often is even in earnest prayer — the saints asked, but scarcely expected to receive. It is a comfort to weak believers, that God hears even when our faith falters — and a rebuke to the unbelief that prays yet does not look to receive.
16:
On Acts 12:16 — “But Peter continued knocking: and when they had opened the door, and saw him, they were astonished” — the witnesses mark the church’s amazement.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “continued knocking” (imperfect) — “now all heard the knocking”; “the whole group rushed out… to make sure” and “were amazed.” Gill notes Peter kept knocking, impatient and in danger of being retaken. Barnes: “now convinced that it was Peter… amazed that he had been rescued.”

The verse ends the suspense at the door. Peter keeps knocking until at last they open and see him; and they are astonished. The disbelief gives way to the undeniable sight of the freed apostle.

So the witnesses see the church’s unbelief overcome by the plain fact. The witnesses note that Peter, left standing outside, simply keeps knocking until the whole company comes and opens the door — and there he stands, beyond all their doubting. Their astonishment, the witnesses observe, is the measure of how little they had expected the answer to their own prayers. They mark Gill’s practical point that Peter’s persistence at the door was urgent, for he stood in danger of recapture should an enemy pass. The verse thus resolves the charming domestic interlude: the praying saints, who had thought Rhoda mad and the news impossible, now see Peter with their own eyes and are amazed. It is a quiet vindication of God’s faithfulness over human unbelief — the answer to prayer standing visibly before those who could not believe it had come — and it prepares Peter’s account of how the Lord had brought him out.

So the witnesses see the church’s unbelief overcome by the plain fact. Peter, left standing outside, simply keeps knocking until the whole company comes and opens the door — and there he stands, beyond all their doubting. Their astonishment is the measure of how little they had expected the answer to their own prayers. They mark Gill’s practical point that Peter’s persistence was urgent, for he stood in danger of recapture. It is a quiet vindication of God’s faithfulness over human unbelief — the answer to prayer standing visibly before those who could not believe it had come.
17:
On Acts 12:17 — “But he, beckoning unto them with the hand to hold their peace, declared… how the Lord had brought him out of the prison. And he said, Go shew these things unto James, and to the brethren” — the witnesses mark Peter’s account and departure.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the gesture and the James. Robertson: “beckoning with the hand” (kataseisas), signalling for silence amid the joyful clamour. On “James,” Ellicott and Wesley take him as James the Lord’s brother, “chief overseer” of the Jerusalem church and author of the epistle. Clarke: “show them to the Church.” The Reformed Calvin notes Peter would have his fellow-apostles certified of the miracle.

The verse gives Peter’s calm report and prudent departure. Quieting their excited joy, he tells how the Lord brought him out, asks that James and the brethren be informed, and then leaves for another place — ascribing all to God and seeking safety.

So the witnesses see Peter give God the glory and act with wise caution. The witnesses note that he hushes the church’s noisy joy, lest it draw the neighbours’ notice, and recounts plainly “how the Lord had brought him out” — crediting not his own courage but God’s power. They mark his thoughtfulness in sending word to James — whom most take to be James the Lord’s brother, now the leading figure of the Jerusalem church — and to the brethren, that the whole church might know and rejoice. They observe his prudence in then withdrawing to “another place,” not tempting God by lingering where Herod could retake him; deliverance does not excuse recklessness. The verse thus shows the freed apostle modest, grateful, and wise: he glorifies God for the rescue, provides for the church’s information, and removes himself from danger — the proper conduct of one whom God has delivered, neither presuming on the miracle nor failing to honour its Author.

So the witnesses see Peter give God the glory and act with wise caution. He hushes the church’s noisy joy, lest it draw notice, and recounts plainly “how the Lord had brought him out” — crediting not his own courage but God’s power. They mark his thoughtfulness in sending word to James and the brethren, and his prudence in withdrawing to “another place,” not tempting God by lingering where Herod could retake him. The freed apostle is modest, grateful, and wise: he glorifies God for the rescue, provides for the church’s information, and removes himself from danger.
18:
On Acts 12:18 — “Now as soon as it was day, there was no small stir among the soldiers, what was become of Peter” — the witnesses mark the consternation of the guards.

The Eastern fathers raise the hard question. Chrysostom: some are at a loss why God let the soldiers be punished for Peter — “it was not yet the time of judgment, so as to render to each according to his deserts.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “no small stir” (litotes); probably all sixteen soldiers were thrown into confusion at dawn. Barnes: “amazement that he had escaped, and apprehension of the consequences.”

The verse turns to the prison at daybreak. When morning comes and Peter is gone, the soldiers are thrown into uproar, unable to explain his disappearance. The guard that had held him so securely is left in panic and dread.

So the witnesses see the confusion in Herod’s prison as the other face of the deliverance. The witnesses note the irony: the sixteen soldiers who had chained and watched Peter find him vanished, the chains in place, the doors secure — no human explanation possible. Their “no small stir” is the amazement and fear of men who know that the escape of a prisoner means their own death under Roman law. The witnesses mark Chrysostom’s grappling with the hard question of why the guards should suffer for a divine deliverance — answering that the final judgment, where each is rendered his due, was not yet. The verse thus shows the consternation that God’s wonders cause among their enemies: what was glory and answered prayer to the church is bewilderment and ruin to the persecutor’s servants. It prepares the next verse, where Herod, unable to explain the escape, vents his rage on the keepers — and it sets the human cost of the miracle within the larger frame of God’s justice, yet to be fully revealed.

So the witnesses see the confusion in Herod’s prison as the other face of the deliverance. The sixteen soldiers who had chained and watched Peter find him vanished, the chains in place, the doors secure — no human explanation possible. Their “no small stir” is the amazement and fear of men who know that the escape of a prisoner means their own death. They mark Chrysostom’s grappling with why the guards should suffer — answering that the final judgment was not yet. What was glory and answered prayer to the church is bewilderment and ruin to the persecutor’s servants.
19:
On Acts 12:19 — “And when Herod had sought for him, and found him not, he examined the keepers, and commanded that they should be put to death… and there abode” — the witnesses mark Herod’s cruelty and retreat.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the law and the rage. Robertson: “examined” (anakrinas), a forensic sifting; “put to death” (led away to execution). Ellicott: capital punishment was “the almost inevitable penalty” under Roman usage for letting a prisoner escape. Wesley: “the wicked suffered in the room of the righteous,” and Herod went down to Caesarea “with shame.” Clarke: jailers “ordinarily suffered the same kind of punishment” due the prisoner.

The verse records Herod’s response to the escape. Unable to find Peter, he interrogates the keepers and has them executed, then withdraws to Caesarea. The frustrated tyrant vents his fury on his own soldiers and leaves Jerusalem in defeat.

So the witnesses see the persecutor baffled and the innocent guards destroyed. The witnesses note that Herod, after a forensic examination, condemns the keepers to death — the standard Roman penalty for a prisoner’s escape — though they were powerless against a divine deliverance. They mark Wesley’s grim observation that “the wicked suffered in the room of the righteous,” the soldiers dying for the apostle they could not have held. They observe that Herod, foiled in his design and shamed before the people he had hoped to please, goes down to Caesarea — his retreat the first sign of the downfall the chapter will complete. The verse thus shows the impotence of even a king against the purposes of God: Herod can murder his guards but cannot reach Peter; his power, which seemed absolute, has been overruled. And his withdrawal to Caesarea sets the scene for the final act — the proud king’s sudden death under the very hand of God that had delivered his prisoner.

So the witnesses see the persecutor baffled and the innocent guards destroyed. Herod, after a forensic examination, condemns the keepers to death — the standard Roman penalty — though they were powerless against a divine deliverance. They mark Wesley’s grim observation that “the wicked suffered in the room of the righteous.” Herod, foiled and shamed before the people he had hoped to please, goes down to Caesarea — his retreat the first sign of the downfall the chapter will complete. He can murder his guards but cannot reach Peter; his power, which seemed absolute, has been overruled.
20:
On Acts 12:20 — “And Herod was highly displeased with them of Tyre and Sidon… they desired peace; because their country was nourished by the king’s country” — the witnesses mark the dispute with the Phoenician cities.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the quarrel. Robertson: “highly displeased” (thymomachon), to fight desperately, a hot quarrel. Ellicott: Tyre and Sidon, under Rome with nominal independence, “were seeking peace.” Lightfoot recalls Ezekiel 27:17 — the Phoenician cities fed from Israel’s land. Wesley: through Blastus they obtained “reconciliation”; the dependence on the king’s country for food gave Herod leverage.

The verse opens the final scene with a political dispute. Herod is at bitter enmity with Tyre and Sidon; they, dependent on his territory for food, seek peace through his chamberlain Blastus. The proud king holds the leverage of their bread.

So the witnesses see the stage set for Herod’s downfall by a matter of state. The witnesses note that the Phoenician cities, though nominally free under Rome, depended on Herod’s realm for their corn, and so could not afford his hostility; they sue for peace, winning over his chamberlain. The witnesses mark Calvin’s anticipation — that this history shows “what end is prepared for the enemies of the Church” and how God hates pride, of which Herod will be the “lively image.” They observe the occasion the reconciliation provided: it gave Herod the platform of a public oration, the very moment of his fatal self-exaltation. The verse thus moves the narrative from the prison to the palace, from the deliverance of Peter to the judgment of the persecutor. The political dealings with Tyre and Sidon, trivial in themselves, become the providential setting in which the proud king, lifted up by flattery, will be struck down by the angel of the Lord.

So the witnesses see the stage set for Herod’s downfall by a matter of state. The Phoenician cities, dependent on Herod’s realm for their corn, sue for peace, winning over his chamberlain. The witnesses mark Calvin’s anticipation — that this history shows what end awaits the enemies of the Church and how God hates pride, of which Herod will be the lively image. They observe the occasion the reconciliation provided: the platform of a public oration, the very moment of his fatal self-exaltation. The narrative moves from the prison to the palace, from the deliverance of Peter to the judgment of the persecutor.
21:
On Acts 12:21 — “And upon a set day Herod, arrayed in royal apparel, sat upon his throne, and made an oration unto them” — the witnesses mark the king in his glory.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw on Josephus. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson: the “set day” was the second day of games at Caesarea in honour of Claudius; the scene is told fully by Josephus (Antiquities xix). Wesley: Herod wore “a garment so wrought with silver, that the rays of the rising sun… dazzled the eyes of the beholders.”

The verse shows Herod at the height of his pomp. On the appointed day, robed in glittering royal apparel, he sits enthroned and addresses the assembly. The persecutor of the church appears in all the splendour of earthly majesty — the very moment before his fall.

So the witnesses see the proud king ascend to the summit of his glory. The witnesses note the corroboration of Josephus, whose independent account of the games at Caesarea, the dazzling silver robe catching the morning sun, and the flattery and sudden death matches Luke’s narrative point for point — a striking confirmation of the sacred history. They mark the picture of human grandeur at its zenith: the king enthroned, robed in light, holding forth before an admiring crowd. The witnesses observe the deliberate contrast the chapter draws — the apostle lately in chains, now free; the king lately triumphant, about to be struck down. The verse thus sets Herod on the pinnacle from which he will fall: arrayed in borrowed splendour, basking in the moment, he stands ripe for the judgment that pride invites. His royal apparel and throne, the emblems of his power, become the backdrop of the divine stroke that will, in two verses, lay the persecutor in the dust.

So the witnesses see the proud king ascend to the summit of his glory. They note the corroboration of Josephus, whose independent account of the games at Caesarea, the dazzling silver robe catching the morning sun, and the flattery and sudden death matches Luke point for point. The picture of human grandeur at its zenith: the king enthroned, robed in light, holding forth before an admiring crowd. The witnesses observe the deliberate contrast — the apostle lately in chains, now free; the king lately triumphant, about to be struck down. His royal apparel becomes the backdrop of the divine stroke.
22:
On Acts 12:22 — “And the people gave a shout, saying, It is the voice of a god, and not of a man” — the witnesses mark the blasphemous flattery.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the heathen acclamation. Robertson: the crowd “kept on shouting” (imperfect), repeating their flattery “to gain Herod’s favour”; “the voice of a god” in the pagan sense of emperor-worship. Barnes: “not probable that the Jews joined… but… the idolatrous Gentiles,” matching Josephus. Gill quotes the flatterers’ cry, hailing him “somewhat more excellent than mortal nature.”

The verse records the crowd’s idolatrous shout. They acclaim Herod’s speech as the voice of a god, not a man — gross flattery in the pagan style of emperor-worship. The king is offered, and does not refuse, the honour due to God alone.

So the witnesses see the fatal flattery that seals Herod’s doom. The witnesses note that the shout came from the idolatrous Gentile crowd, eager to win the king’s favour by hailing him as divine — the language of pagan emperor-worship, ascribing to a man the honour of God. They mark the agreement with Josephus, whose account of the flatterers’ cry confirms Luke’s. The witnesses observe the heart of Herod’s sin, which the next verse names: not that the crowd flattered, but that he received their blasphemy without rebuke, usurping the glory of God. The verse thus brings the proud king to the brink of judgment: arrayed in splendour, lauded as a god, he stands at the very summit of self-exaltation. The acclamation that gratified his vanity is in truth the snare of his destruction — for the God whose glory he allowed to be given to himself will not share his honour, and the stroke is about to fall.

So the witnesses see the fatal flattery that seals Herod’s doom. The shout came from the idolatrous Gentile crowd, eager to win the king’s favour by hailing him as divine — the language of pagan emperor-worship, ascribing to a man the honour of God. They mark the agreement with Josephus. The heart of Herod’s sin, which the next verse names, is not that the crowd flattered, but that he received their blasphemy without rebuke, usurping the glory of God. The acclamation that gratified his vanity is in truth the snare of his destruction — for God will not share his honour.
23:
On Acts 12:23 — “And immediately the angel of the Lord smote him, because he gave not God the glory: and he was eaten of worms, and gave up the ghost” — the witnesses mark the judgment of God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the stroke and its cause. Robertson: “smote him” (epataxen, the same verb as the gentle smiting of verse 7, here severe); “like Nebuchadnezzar… pride went before a fall” (Daniel 4:30). Clarke: “because he gave not God the glory… he did not rebuke his flatterers.” Ellicott sees “the law of Nemesis” and divine judgment. The Reformed Calvin: God “resisteth the proud” (1 Peter 5:5), Herod a “lively image” thereof. Lightfoot and Gill note Josephus’s parallel account of his agony.

The verse delivers the swift judgment. The same angel who freed Peter now smites the proud king; struck for usurping God’s glory, he is consumed by worms and dies. The persecutor falls at the height of his arrogance.

So the witnesses see in Herod’s death the manifest judgment of God on pride. The witnesses note the pointed cause — “because he gave not God the glory”: not merely that he was flattered, but that he accepted divine honour without protest, robbing God. They mark the dread irony Robertson and Ellicott press: the angel who had gently touched Peter to free him now strikes Herod to destroy him; the hand that delivered the saint smites the persecutor. They observe the corroboration of Josephus, whose independent record of Herod’s sudden agony at Caesarea confirms the sacred account, and the loathsome manner — eaten of worms — befitting one who had exalted himself as a god. Calvin reads it as the lively proof that God resists the proud. The verse thus completes the chapter’s great reversal: the king who stretched out his hand against the church, slew James, and would have slain Peter, is himself struck down by the angel of the Lord — a warning to all who usurp the glory of God.

So the witnesses see in Herod’s death the manifest judgment of God on pride. The pointed cause — “because he gave not God the glory”: not merely that he was flattered, but that he accepted divine honour without protest, robbing God. They mark the dread irony: the angel who had gently touched Peter to free him now strikes Herod to destroy him; the hand that delivered the saint smites the persecutor. They observe the corroboration of Josephus and the loathsome manner, eaten of worms, befitting one who exalted himself as a god — the lively proof that God resists the proud.
24:
On Acts 12:24 — “But the word of God grew and multiplied” — the witnesses mark the triumph of the gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: “grew and multiplied” (imperfect), “the reaction from the death of James and the imprisonment of Peter.” Ellicott: “the death of the chief persecutor left free scope for the… preachers.” The Reformed Calvin: “when the tyrant was once taken out of the way the Church was suddenly delivered… the Church doth always overlive her enemies.”

The verse sets the flourishing of the gospel against the fall of its persecutor. While Herod is dead and gone, the word of God grows and multiplies. The cause the king sought to crush thrives the more for his removal.

So the witnesses see the chapter close on the triumph of the word over its enemies. The witnesses note the pointed contrast: Herod, who stretched out his hand against the church, is eaten of worms and dead; but the word of God grows and multiplies, unhindered and increasing. They mark Calvin’s great theme — that the church always outlives her persecutors; the tyrant falls, the gospel advances. The death of the chief persecutor, Ellicott observes, gave free scope to the preachers, so that the very removal of Herod furthered the cause he had attacked. The witnesses observe the divine pattern displayed through the whole chapter: an apostle martyred, another imprisoned and freed, a persecutor struck down, and the word triumphing over all. The verse thus crowns the narrative with its abiding lesson: no king, no chains, no sword can stop the gospel; what man means to crush, God makes to grow. The cause of Christ is invincible, multiplying even where its enemies rage and over the graves of those who oppose it.

So the witnesses see the chapter close on the triumph of the word over its enemies. The pointed contrast: Herod, who stretched out his hand against the church, is eaten of worms and dead; but the word of God grows and multiplies, unhindered. They mark Calvin’s great theme — that the church always outlives her persecutors; the tyrant falls, the gospel advances. The death of the chief persecutor gave free scope to the preachers, so that his very removal furthered the cause he attacked. No king, no chains, no sword can stop the gospel; what man means to crush, God makes to grow.
25:
On Acts 12:25 — “And Barnabas and Saul returned from Jerusalem, when they had fulfilled their ministry, and took with them John, whose surname was Mark” — the witnesses mark the return to Antioch.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the errand and the companion. Barnes and Robertson: they had gone to Jerusalem with the alms of Antioch (Acts 11:29–30), and now return, the ministration (the famine relief) fulfilled. Ellicott and Wesley: they took John Mark, Barnabas’s cousin, who would become Paul’s companion (and later occasion of dispute). Calvin: the ministry was “the alms” gathered for the church at Jerusalem.

The verse closes the chapter by resuming the wider narrative. Barnabas and Saul, having delivered the famine relief to Jerusalem, return to Antioch, taking John Mark with them. The thread of the mission, interrupted by Herod’s persecution, is taken up again.

So the witnesses see the chapter end by linking back to the ongoing work of the church. The witnesses note that Barnabas and Saul had come to Jerusalem on an errand of mercy — bearing the alms of Antioch for the famine-stricken brethren — and now, that ministry fulfilled, they return north. They mark the significant detail that they take John Mark, the son of the Mary at whose house the church had prayed, and cousin of Barnabas; the young man who will share, and then abandon, and at last be restored to the missionary work. The witnesses observe how the verse stitches the dramatic episode of Herod and Peter back into the broader story of the gospel’s advance from Antioch. The verse thus turns from the deliverance and judgment just recounted to the next stage of mission: the relief delivered, the leaders returning, a new helper enlisted — the church, having weathered persecution, moving forward into the wider work that the following chapters, with the first missionary journey, will unfold.

So the witnesses see the chapter end by linking back to the ongoing work of the church. Barnabas and Saul had come to Jerusalem on an errand of mercy — bearing the alms of Antioch for the famine-stricken brethren — and now, that ministry fulfilled, they return north. They mark the significant detail that they take John Mark, son of the Mary at whose house the church had prayed, and cousin of Barnabas. The verse stitches the dramatic episode of Herod and Peter back into the broader story of the gospel’s advance: the relief delivered, the leaders returning, a new helper enlisted for the wider work to come.
